content.title: Study on key technologies of GNSS-based train state perception for train-centric railway signaling
content.abstract_or_snippet: . Safe train envelope estimation For GNSS-based train positioning and train integrity monitoring, the uncertainty in state estimation has to be concerned to ensure the safety and efficiency of train operation by utilizing the integrity monitoring result. Under the moving block train control scheme, where the occu- pancy of the track segments is determined by the practical length of the train, it is necessary to add a reasonable margin to the raw HOT and EOT location to form a safe occupancy range [13]. The safe train position has to cover the most unfavorable conditions. With this consideration, the margin should be sufficiently large to realize a high possibility to protect the unsatisfied situations. However, an excessive margin will lead to unreasonable identification of track occupancy, which may greatly af- fect the operational efficiency. To realize the expected balance level between the safety and efficiency characteristics, a constrained safe margin has to be adopted on the basis of safety and rationality, so as to reduce the train tracing interval and improve the system efficiency. The essence of the safe train envelope is to realize the safety as- surance of train control by describing the reliability of train positioning. The safe train envelope extends the positioning output based on the evaluation of the uncertainty of positioning errors, and provides a safe overlay to the most possible position derived by the HOT/EOT positioning unit. The safe margin consists of the HPL by integrity monitoring result and the confidential interval of the positioning error. The HPL, which indicates the radius of a circle in the horizontal plane with its center being at the true position to describe the region assured to contain the indicated horizontal position, is usually regarded as the safety boundary of positioning result by GNSS. However, the existence of the integrity risk may lead to an unbounded position error that exceeds the HPL. Without the high-level referencing system during the dynamic operation, it is difficult to accurately evaluate the relia- bility of the safety boundary by HPL. In order to guarantee the cred- ibility of the safe margin for envelope evaluation, it is necessary to estimate the possible range of the positioning error by a specific error confidence interval strategy. Considering the track-constrained along- track operation of railway trains, the error confidence interval indicates a one-dimensional along-track interval that can be represented by the uncertainties in state prediction, measurement update and the trackmap-based location determination, which means (4) and where confidence interval at instant k, result, geographic information from the trackmap database. represent the lower and upper limits of the indicates the user measurement represents the prior denotes the prediction result, and

The main contributions of this paper are as follows. 1) A novel CPN-based modeling framework for GNSS-based train po- sitioning systems is developed. 2) Dynamic influences of signal interferences (i.e., by AM signals, FM signals, pulse signals) and environmental obstructions (i.e., by mountains and tunnels) are incorporated into the model. 3) The performance of the GNSS-based train positioning system is quantitatively evaluated under various scenarios with the CPN model of the system. 2. Related works Over the past decades, GNSS-based systems have been extensively ap- plied to train positioning due to their global coverage and high accuracy under favorable conditions [1,7]. Despite these advantages, challenges such as signal interferences and terrain-induced occlusions continue to pose questions on high positioning accuracy and stability. Numerous studies have explored techniques to mitigate the impacts of signal interferences on GNSS- based positioning accuracy [8–12]. For example, Ref. [9] investigates the impact of continuous wave interference on GNSS receiver tracking perfor- mance, examining the extent of its influence by varying data bit durations or integration times. To address interference-related challenges, an inter- ference energy detection method and a comprehensive orientation tech- nology tailored for GNSS are introduced in Ref. [11]. Besides, a number of researches have examined the impact of environmental factors along railway lines on GNSS signal integrity. For instance, Ref. [13] explores methods of scenario-based weighted measurement noise calculation and fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua- tion in autonomous train positioning systems. Some studies have achieved high positioning accuracy in partially obstructed or challenging environ- ments by fusing multi-sensor data [6,

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 116–124 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article , Wenzheng Qiaoa, Baigen Caia,b,c, Jian Wanga,b,c, Jiang Liua,b,c, Yuchen Zhaoa, Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways Debiao Lua,b,c,⁎ Chunjie Qiaod, Dirk Spiegele a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c Beijing Engineering Center for EMC and GNSS Technologies, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China d Hunan Matrix Electronic Technology Co., Ltd., Changsha 410205, China e DB InfraGO AG, Berlin 10557, Germany ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS testing Railway train localization 3D environment modelling Digital track map Zero on-site testing The integration of Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) technology into railway train control systems is a crucial step toward achieving the vision of a digital railway. Traditional train control systems undergo extensive in-house tests and prolonged field tests for certification and approval before operational deployment, leading to high costs, delays, and operational disruptions. This paper introduces a GNSS-based train control localization framework which eliminates the need for on-site testing by leveraging train movement dynamics and 3D en- vironment modeling to create a zero on-site testing platform. The proposed framework simulates train move- ment and the surrounding 3D environment using collected railway line location data and environmental attri- butes to generate realistic multipath signals and obscuration effects. This approach enables comprehensive laboratory-based case studies for train localization, reducing the huge amount test of needed for physical field trials. The framework is established in house, using the data collected at the Test Base of China Academy of Railway Sciences (Circular Railway). Results from the open area and cutting environment tests demonstrate high localization accuracy repeatability within the simulated environment, validating the feasibility and effectiveness of zero on-site testing for GNSS-based train control systems. This research highlights the potential of GNSS simulation platforms in enhancing cost efficiency, operational safety, and accuracy for future digital railways.

Conclusion In this paper, a GNSS jamming protection solution is presented based on the INS-aided deep integration solution. The solution enables a self-contained PVT reference that is immune to RF signals. INS in- formation is sent to the receiver’s baseband, realizing the assistance to the receiver at the signal processing level. The measured velocity in- formation from INS is mapped to the LOS between the satellite and the vehicle to obtain the Doppler of relative motion. The Doppler is fed back to the NCO in the tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The the proposed solution demonstrates significant evaluation of improvements in positioning and velocity, enabling resilient train po- sitioning for securing next generation GNSS-based train control sys- tems. Future work will focus on refining mitigation techniques with extended studies for multi-constellation scenarios (e.g., BeiDou and Galileo), and exploring field tests in real lines to ensure the resilience of GNSS-based train localization systems in railway applications. content.title: Study on key technologies of GNSS-based train state perception for train-centric railway signaling
content.abstract_or_snippet: data volume, which releases the require- ment to the train-ground communication and the complexity of system interfaces for trackmap verification. Through the integration of static trackmap information, sensor data fusion for BDS/GPS re- ceivers, INS sensors and the odometer measurements were real- timely achieved with a high availability level under the complex and time-changing operation environment. Both the static and dynamic test cases were involved in the field demonstration. Table 6 sum- marizes the accuracy results of train positioning under different system modes. The results from field demonstration illustrate that involvement of the presented key technologies, including the safe platform and multi- source sensor fusion mechanism, effectively guarantees the perfor- mance of GNSS-based autonomous train positioning and state percep- tion even under complex and time-varying operational conditions. The utilization of the differential GNSS technology and the integrity mon- itoring logic makes it possible of achieving the high accuracy and ro- bustness against the possible difficult or even critical conditions. The field test results demonstrate the feasibility and the advantages of utilizing BDS in realizing a novel autonomous train control system with a train-centric design, which is of great significance in the future high- speed and plateau railway applications with the enhanced environ- mental flexibility and whole life cycle cost efficiency. 7. Conclusion The application of GNSS technology is one of the representative characteristics of intelligent railway systems in the future. However, there are specific requirements to the GNSS-based train positioning and state perception in the railway signaling applications due to the critical re- quirement to the safety. Thus, the utilization of GNSS has to be enhanced by the specific technical solutions. The sensor data fusion technique is of great significance to enhance the service availability under degraded or even a critical GNSS observing condition. To ensure the compatibility to the existing system specifications, the VB technology enables the in- tegration of the GNSS capabilities into the train control system and meanwhile the train integrity monitoring logic allows the opportunity to realize moving block-based train operation control through the dynamic identification of the track occupancy and safe envelope of the train. By investigating the relationship between the GNSS performance system and the RAMS specification, the performance evaluation of the GNSS-based train positioning can be effectively realized. It can be found that the in- volvement of these key technologies consolidates the autonomy and cap- abilities of the train-borne system, which releases the requirement to the track-side facilities like track circuit and Balises. This research highlights the potential of GNSS simulation platforms in enhancing cost efficiency, operational safety, and accuracy for future digital railways. 1. Introduction Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) have seen vast applications in the transportation domain. The integration of Global Positioning System (GPS) and Beidou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) in railway is efficient for modernizing the railway network, GNSS is increasingly becoming a fundamental pillar not only for non-safety applications, but also for im- proved maintenance, automated train operation, and safety- related appli- cations, including the Next Generation of Train Control (NGTC) and re- novation of the train on-board equipment, all these will increase the railway system capacity, efficiency and safety of all railways [1]. To apply GNSS in railway train operation, the GNSS performance testing and verification is the essential procedure for the system to be adopted and further listed as mandatory in the system design and development. The certification of the GNSS-based train localization systems is also necessary to provide concrete validation or testing application services to carry out simulation tests with evaluation of the platform and environmental condi- tion simulations [2]. Beidou has a set of technical and regulatory standards, including the BDS Performance Standard, BDS Signal Interface Control Document (ICD), and technical requirements for user equipment. The BDS Signal In Space (SIS) and service performance characteristics include ac- curacy, availability, compatibility, and interoperability [3]. GPS has also published standard positioning service performance standards to guide the user for civil uses worldwide, the performance metrics are GPS SIS accuracy, continuity, and availability [4]. So both BDS and GPS are focusing on ac- curacy as the foundation for the GNSS performance [5]. The performance needs to be assessed and as the performances are all credited to the equipment, the equipment itself needs to be certified. The certification procedure, it is governed by various international and regional standards, such as the accuracy standards by BDS and GPS, the test and simulation standard procedures, the simulation must meet possible (as sensible to the GNSS) to make the test results plausible. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex- tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems. Early in the 1990s, the United States introduces the concept of the Positive Train control System (PTS) [1], which relies on Global Posi- tioning System (GPS), accelerometers, and fiber-optic gyroscopes for the train positioning function. By December 2020, the United States achieved 100 % implementation of Positive Train Control (PTC) across all required railroads, marking a historic milestone in rail safety and interoperability [2]. CLUG 2.0 project [3] launched in 2023 in Europe aims to enhance the European Rail Traffic Management System (ERTMS) by integrating an on-board GNSS+EGNOS-based multi-sensor fusion architecture. In China, the China State Railway Group Co., Ltd. introduced a new train control system, the Chinese Train Control System-New (CTCS-N) [4], which proposed a “train-borne centric” train operation control method. This system incorporates on-board GNSS positioning, speed sensors, and electronic railway maps to achieve train localization. GNSS has been acknowledged as a pivotal enabler in im- proving the cost efficiency and automation level of the train control system. In complex railway operation scenarios, such as urban canyons, tunnels, and stations with cover, the limited GNSS signal visibility, and weak observation geometry would greatly decrease the positioning performance. Thus, the integration solutions are typically proposed for the train safety positioning. A typical integration solution for GNSS- based train positioning is based on the data fusion with both GNSS and Inertial Navigation System (INS) sensors. The GNSS/INS integrated solution is capable of providing full navigation solutions of position, velocity, and attitude in high positioning accuracy, high updating rate even under the GNSS limited scenario when the visible satellites are less than four. Additionally, there is an increasing concern that GNSS is also vulnerable to radio frequency jamming, due to the weak signal power of the GNSS signal. More and more GNSS disturbance events are reported because of jamming, in worldwide, including maritime applications [5,6], aviation applications [7], and also railway applications [8,9]. GNSS in-band interferences, for example, intentional jamming shows a source of potential threats to the safe, secure, and resilient operation of a dense railway system utilizing GNSS as the main positioning method

content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning Zhuojian Caoa, Jiang Liua,b,c,⁎ a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b State Key Laboratory of Advanced Rail Autonomous Operation, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China , Wei Jianga,b,c, Baigen Caia,b,c ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS jamming Deeply-coupled integration GNSS/INS Railway safety and efficiency increasingly rely on precise train positioning. The integration of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) into railway control systems aims to reduce dependence on track-side in- frastructure. While GNSS has significantly improved train localization, challenges such as the susceptibility to jamming remain. To address this, this paper introduces an Inertial Navigation System (INS)-aided train posi- tioning system based on deep integration, exploring its performance through semi-physical experiments and simulations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed solution is able to reduce the positioning error by 63.47 %, and the velocity error by 58.47 % under jamming conditions. The study highlights the potential of deep integration for improving the resilience of GNSS-based train control systems, especially in the face of Radio Frequency (RF) jamming threats. 1. Introduction Railway safety and efficiency depend increasingly on knowing every train’s position. Traditional train positioning technologies usually in- volve track circuit positioning, axle counter positioning, and intersec- tion inductive loop positioning. However, these methods are often limited by insufficient positioning accuracy and high maintenance costs. The application of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) in railway train control systems aims to reduce as much as possible the use of track-side infrastructures for train location awareness, including track circuits and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex- tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals, which makes it possible to benefit from the mitigation of GNSS jamming signals. In the railway domain, an extensive analysis of interference events based on a one-year dataset collected across the Swiss railway network was con- ducted in Ref. [8], which revealed widespread occurrences of GNSS interference, particularly clustered around larger cities and densely populated residential areas. A test and evaluation of GNSS-based railway positioning under various jamming conditions was presented in Ref. [17], demonstrating the susceptibility of train localization perfor- mance to interference. A resilient localization framework operating at the measurement level, incorporating jamming discrimination and multi-sensor fusion, was proposed in Ref. [18]. Nevertheless, research on GNSS anti-jamming solutions for railway environments remains re- latively limited, which motivates the focus of the present work. GNSS/INS integration can be categorized into three primary archi- tectures: loosely coupled, tightly coupled, and deeply coupled sys- tems [19,20]. In a loosely coupled integration, the GNSS receiver and INS operate independently, with integration occurring at the navigation solution level by combining the position and velocity outputs from both systems. This approach requires a minimum of four visible satellites for a positioning solution, which can be a limitation in environments with jamming signals. In contrast, tightly-coupled integration integrates raw GNSS measurements, such as pseudoranges and Doppler shifts, directly with INS solution within a filter. This method allows the system to utilize all available satellite signals, even when fewer than four sa- tellites are visible, enhancing performance in challenging environ- ments. Deeply coupled, or ultra-tight, integration goes a step further by incorporating inertial measurements directly into the GNSS signal tracking loops. This integration aids in maintaining signal lock under high dynamics and interference conditions, as the INS can provide predictive information to the GNSS receiver’s tracking algorithms. The advantages of deep integration are particularly evident in environments with significant interference or high dynamics. In Ref. [21], the anti- jamming performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-j

content.title: Study on key technologies of GNSS-based train state perception for train-centric railway signaling
content.abstract_or_snippet: will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. GNSS/INS/Odometer sensor data fusion The ODO (Odometer) is the most commonly used train positioning method in existing train control systems. Wheel slide and slip during the train operation, as well as wheel diameter wear, may result in errors in the train speed measurement, and lead to error accumulation in the along-track location calculation results. Such errors will be brought into the INS/ODO integrated positioning system. The train positioning error by INS/ODO drifts over time until the position calibration results are available. Different from the Balises, results of GNSS positioning can provide the correction without relying on the track-side. Performance of a GNSS/INS/ODO integrated positioning system will be determined by the reliability and accuracy of GNSS. When the GNSS performance decreases or the GNSS signal is blocked for a long time, the result of INS will not be guaranteed effectively [6]. Therefore, the availability and observation quality of GNSS greatly affect the precision and effective- ness of train positioning using INS and ODO. The key issue in realizing the multi-sensor integrated train positioning is the sensor fusion logic. It is in charge of combining the measurements from multiple sensors, improving the reliability and fault tolerance of the integrated system, providing suitable auxiliary information for the ex- isting location determination system, and simplifying the system com- plexity over those track-side-relied solutions. Among different sensor fu- sion algorithms, Federated Kalman Filter (FKF) is a distributed cascaded integrated sensor fusion method and widely considered in applications because of its flexibility, low computational effort, and excellent fault tolerance capability. FKF consists of several local filters and a main filter, in which the system information is allocated in the local filters and then fused in the main filter. For the GNSS/INS/ODO integrated train posi- tioning system based on the FKF, INS is integrated due to its advantages in high output rate, autonomy and reliability. Considering the INS-related local filtering structures, two local filters for GNSS/INS and INS/ODO can 48 Fig. 1. Architecture of GNSS/INS/ODO sensor data fusion for integrated train positioning. be established, as shown in Fig. 1. Based on this traditional tightly-coupled GNSS/INS integra- tion, the estimated navigation states are used to estimate the Doppler shift information, which is fed back to the GNSS tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The system operates in a closed-loop way where the integrated filter continuously updates the system’s state estimation by combining data from both the GNSS and INS, and the feedback of the estimated states helps improve the accuracy of both the INS and GNSS calculations. The INS-aided deep integration system leverages the in- ertial data, effectively narrowing the bandwidth of the tracking loop, which helps reduce the noise within the loop and suppress external jamming signals. The system’s capability to maintain accurate tracking and position estimation in jamming environments is significantly strengthened, leading to a more resilient navigation solution. 3. Jamming analysis and protection 3.1. Jamming analysis The received signal strength of the GNSS receiver is extremely low and falls well below the thermal noise floor. Typically, the received GPS signal levels fall within the range of −127 dBm [22]. The weak char- acteristics of GNSS signals will lead to an increasing risk of performance degradation under jamming conditions. Jamming signals may emanate from telecommunication and electronic systems operating in adjacent bands or in bands relatively far from the GNSS bands, i.e., Fre- quency Modulation (FM)/TV transmitter harmonics, Amplitude Mod- ulation (AM) transmitters, and mobile phone networks. content.abstract_or_snippet: performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-jamming methods remains constrained by simple jamming models such as con- tinuous wave jamming. The mechanisms under different jamming modulation schemes remain underexplored. This study expands the exploration of jamming types by focusing on sweep-frequency inter- ference. Considering the compatibility of the GNSS/INS deeply-coupled integration with the current GNSS-based train positioning function in the control system conception, this paper proposes an INS-aided train determination system (LDS) based on the deep integration. The system profile and the GNSS jamming signals are briefly introduced firstly. Then, we give a detailed introduction of deeply-coupled integration design. Finally, a hardware simulator is used and semi-physical ex- periments are conducted to assess the proposed system’s performance with a sweep frequency jamming scenario. 2. System design From Fig. 1, it can be found that multiple information resources are involved in the train positioning system, where the inertial data from the accelerometers and gyroscopes is taken into the Position, Velocity, and Timing (PVT) calculation to enhance the resilience to abnormal jamming attacks. By integrating an INS directly into the train onboard subsystem, it becomes unnecessary to install or modify the track-side subsystem, thereby simplifying system design and maintenance, thereby providing a flexible architecture for modern rail train control systems. From a signal processing perspective, jamming signals are collected and analyzed together with the authentic GNSS signals by the antenna and the subsequent front-end stage in the GNSS receiver simulta- neously. The signal contaminated by jamming signals would directly affect the subsequent stages of the receiver’s processing chain, in- cluding acquisition and tracking stages. In the tracking stage, jamming prevents the code and carrier loops from maintaining stable signal tracking, ultimately resulting in pronounced errors or even failure in the PVT solution. In the proposed system, the Doppler shift information derived from INS is fed back to aid the GNSS tracking loop, improving the overall resilience of the navigation solution. Inertial mechanization is used to infer Inertial Solution at discrete epochs, while the GNSS tracking loop operates simultaneously until they meet the integration epoch. hypotheses.statement: Im Bahnkontext ist GNSS allein in komplexen Umgebungen und bei Funkstörungen nicht hinreichend robust; insbesondere Tunnel, Einschnitte/Half-sky-Szenarien, Terrainabschattung und AM/FM/Puls-Interferenzen verschlechtern Sichtbarkeit, Signalverfolgung und Positionsgenauigkeit bis hin zum vollständigen Ausfall im Tunnel.
hypotheses.reasoning: Mehrere Dokumente zeigen konsistent, dass Umweltobstruktionen und Interferenzen die GNSS-Leistung stark degradieren: modellierte Bahnszenarien mit Tunnelabschirmung, Terrainblockade und AM/FM/Puls-Störungen verschlechtern die Genauigkeit signifikant; im Tunnel werden Signale vollständig blockiert und die Wiedererfassung nach dem Tunnelausgang verursacht große Fehler. Typische Bahnumgebungen werden explizit als open area, half-sky, cutting und tunnel unterschieden. Zusätzlich belegt eine Bahn-Jamming-Analyse die besondere Verwundbarkeit schwacher GNSS-Signale gegenüber Störungen.

hypotheses.statement: Die Integration von INS erhöht die Resilienz der Zugortung, weil inertiale Messungen GNSS-Ausfälle, reduzierte Satellitensichtbarkeit und Jamming auf System- bzw. Tracking-Ebene überbrücken; besonders tief gekoppelte GNSS/INS-Architekturen stabilisieren die Signalverfolgung und senken Fehler unter Störbedingungen deutlich.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die INS-Dokumente beschreiben, dass tief gekoppelte GNSS/INS-Systeme inertiale Informationen direkt in Tracking-Loops einspeisen, Doppler-Vorhersagen liefern und dadurch Signal-Lock und Positionsschätzung unter Jamming robuster machen; Experimente berichten Fehlerreduktionen von 63,47 % bei Position und 58,47 % bei Geschwindigkeit. Feldtests zur Bahnsensorfusion zeigen zudem, dass GNSS/INS/ODO und differenzielles GNSS mit Fusion die dynamische Positionierung verbessern. Gleichzeitig bleibt laut Integrationsdokument die Drift von INS/ODO bei langen GNSS-Ausfällen eine wirksame Randbedingung, sodass INS Resilienz erhöht, GNSS aber nicht beliebig lange ersetzt.

hypotheses.statement: Für ausfallsicheren digitalen Bahnbetrieb genügt GNSS/INS-Genauigkeit allein nicht; erforderlich sind eisenbahnspezifische Integritätsüberwachung, sichere HOT/EOT-Zughüllen mit HPL/Konfidenzintervallen sowie RAMS-orientierte Verfügbarkeits- und Sicherheitsnachweise.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die Dokumente zur train state perception machen deutlich, dass Positionsunsicherheit direkt in Sicherheitsmargen für HOT/EOT, Integritätsbestimmung und sichere Zugbelegung eingeht. Die sichere Zughülle basiert auf HPL und Konfidenzintervallen; zugleich werden GNSS-bezogene Kennzahlen explizit an die RAMS-Architektur angebunden. Felddemonstrationen nennen Integritätslogik und Sensorfusion als Voraussetzung für hohe Verfügbarkeit und Robustheit unter komplexen Betriebsbedingungen.

hypotheses.statement: Die praktische Einführung von GNSS/INS in der digitalen Schiene wird wesentlich durch Validierungs-, Test- und Zertifizierungsanforderungen begrenzt; daher gewinnen standardisierte Labor- und Zero-on-site-Testplattformen mit 3D-Umgebungsmodellierung und Digital Track Maps an Bedeutung, um Feldtests kostengünstiger und risikoärmer zu ersetzen bzw. vorzubereiten.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die Testdokumente betonen, dass GNSS-Leistungstests und Verifikation essenziell und für Zulassung/Zertifizierung notwendig sind. Klassische Feldvalidierung ist ressourcenintensiv, teuer und mit Betriebs- und Sicherheitsrisiken verbunden. Als Gegenansatz beschreiben die Dokumente Zero-on-site-Plattformen, die Zugbewegung, Multipfad und Abschattung in 3D modellieren und reproduzierbare Laborprüfungen für typische Bahnumgebungen ermöglichen.

input.query: Vor welchen Herausforderungen steht der Einsatz von GNSS in der digitalen Schieneninfrastruktur und wie trägt die Integration von Inertialsensoren (INS) dazu bei, einen ausfallsicheren Bahnbetrieb zu gewährleisten?

hypotheses.statement: Im Bahnkontext ist GNSS allein in komplexen Umgebungen und bei Funkstörungen nicht hinreichend robust; insbesondere Tunnel, Einschnitte/Half-sky-Szenarien, Terrainabschattung und AM/FM/Puls-Interferenzen verschlechtern Sichtbarkeit, Signalverfolgung und Positionsgenauigkeit bis hin zum vollständigen Ausfall im Tunnel.
hypotheses.reasoning: Mehrere Dokumente zeigen konsistent, dass Umweltobstruktionen und Interferenzen die GNSS-Leistung stark degradieren: modellierte Bahnszenarien mit Tunnelabschirmung, Terrainblockade und AM/FM/Puls-Störungen verschlechtern die Genauigkeit signifikant; im Tunnel werden Signale vollständig blockiert und die Wiedererfassung nach dem Tunnelausgang verursacht große Fehler. Typische Bahnumgebungen werden explizit als open area, half-sky, cutting und tunnel unterschieden. Zusätzlich belegt eine Bahn-Jamming-Analyse die besondere Verwundbarkeit schwacher GNSS-Signale gegenüber Störungen.

hypotheses.statement: Die Integration von INS erhöht die Resilienz der Zugortung, weil inertiale Messungen GNSS-Ausfälle, reduzierte Satellitensichtbarkeit und Jamming auf System- bzw. Tracking-Ebene überbrücken; besonders tief gekoppelte GNSS/INS-Architekturen stabilisieren die Signalverfolgung und senken Fehler unter Störbedingungen deutlich.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die INS-Dokumente beschreiben, dass tief gekoppelte GNSS/INS-Systeme inertiale Informationen direkt in Tracking-Loops einspeisen, Doppler-Vorhersagen liefern und dadurch Signal-Lock und Positionsschätzung unter Jamming robuster machen; Experimente berichten Fehlerreduktionen von 63,47 % bei Position und 58,47 % bei Geschwindigkeit. Feldtests zur Bahnsensorfusion zeigen zudem, dass GNSS/INS/ODO und differenzielles GNSS mit Fusion die dynamische Positionierung verbessern. Gleichzeitig bleibt laut Integrationsdokument die Drift von INS/ODO bei langen GNSS-Ausfällen eine wirksame Randbedingung, sodass INS Resilienz erhöht, GNSS aber nicht beliebig lange ersetzt.

hypotheses.statement: Für ausfallsicheren digitalen Bahnbetrieb genügt GNSS/INS-Genauigkeit allein nicht; erforderlich sind eisenbahnspezifische Integritätsüberwachung, sichere HOT/EOT-Zughüllen mit HPL/Konfidenzintervallen sowie RAMS-orientierte Verfügbarkeits- und Sicherheitsnachweise.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die Dokumente zur train state perception machen deutlich, dass Positionsunsicherheit direkt in Sicherheitsmargen für HOT/EOT, Integritätsbestimmung und sichere Zugbelegung eingeht. Die sichere Zughülle basiert auf HPL und Konfidenzintervallen; zugleich werden GNSS-bezogene Kennzahlen explizit an die RAMS-Architektur angebunden. Felddemonstrationen nennen Integritätslogik und Sensorfusion als Voraussetzung für hohe Verfügbarkeit und Robustheit unter komplexen Betriebsbedingungen.

hypotheses.statement: Die praktische Einführung von GNSS/INS in der digitalen Schiene wird wesentlich durch Validierungs-, Test- und Zertifizierungsanforderungen begrenzt; daher gewinnen standardisierte Labor- und Zero-on-site-Testplattformen mit 3D-Umgebungsmodellierung und Digital Track Maps an Bedeutung, um Feldtests kostengünstiger und risikoärmer zu ersetzen bzw. vorzubereiten.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die Testdokumente betonen, dass GNSS-Leistungstests und Verifikation essenziell und für Zulassung/Zertifizierung notwendig sind. Klassische Feldvalidierung ist ressourcenintensiv, teuer und mit Betriebs- und Sicherheitsrisiken verbunden. Als Gegenansatz beschreiben die Dokumente Zero-on-site-Plattformen, die Zugbewegung, Multipfad und Abschattung in 3D modellieren und reproduzierbare Laborprüfungen für typische Bahnumgebungen ermöglichen.

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 175–184 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets Shuting Chena,b, Daohua Wua,b,⁎ a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b National Research Center of Railway Safety Assessment, Collaborative Innovation Center of Railway Traffic Safety, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c State Key Laboratory of Advanced Rail Autonomous Operation, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China d Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China , Jiang Liua,c,d, Siqi Wanga,d ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS-based train positioning Positioning performance Environment scenario GNSS signal interference Colored petri Nets Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-based continuous and accurate train positioning is one of the key technologies for advanced train operations such as train virtual coupling. However, GNSS-based train posi- tioning faces significant challenges in real-world scenarios due to environmental complexities and signal in- terferences. Considering this issue, this paper presents an approach for modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning systems using Colored Petri Nets (CPNs). By systematically modeling the GNSS signal reception and processing process, the performance of the positioning system under various environment scenarios is evaluated. The system model integrates three types of interference signals (i.e., Amplitude Modulation (AM) signals, Frequency Modulation (FM) signals, and pulse signals) while incorporating environ- mental factors such as terrain obstructions and tunnel shielding. Additionally, the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) algorithm is employed to process GNSS observation data, providing accurate train position estimations. The simulation results demonstrate that signal interferences and complex environmental conditions significantly affect the GNSS-based positioning accuracy. This study offers a comprehensive framework for evaluating the performance of GNSS-based train positioning systems in different scenarios, highlighting critical factors that influence positioning accuracy and stability. 1. Introduction With the increasing demand for operation safety and efficiency in rail transportation, train virtual coupling has been proposed and be- come a hot research topic in recent years. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: terrain, with the elevation direction being particularly sensitive to these factors. Tunnel scenarios show the most pronounced degradation with a mean error of 5.6670 m. This dramatic increase results from severe signal blockages in tunnels and the instability during signal reacquisition after exiting the tunnel. Fig. 10 illustrates the evolution of position error in the tunnel en- vironment. During the “Inside the Tunnel” phase, GNSS signals are completely blocked by the surrounding structure, resulting in a loss of positioning capability (represented by the gray shaded region). Upon exiting the tunnel (marked by the vertical dashed line), the system begins to reacquire GNSS signals; however, due to severe multipath effects and signal obstructions, the initial position errors are sig- nificantly large. As the train moves further away from the tunnel exit, the surrounding environment improves, leading to a gradual reduction in error, which eventually stabilizes. This trend highlights the critical Fig. 10. Position errors in tunnel scenario. impact of tunnel environments and their transitions on GNSS-based positioning performance. 5. Conclusion This paper presents a CPNs-based approach to evaluate the perfor- mances of GNSS-based train positioning systems under real-world op- erational conditions. By constructing a hierarchical CPN model, the study successfully models the process of GNSS signal reception and interferences, as well as the impacts of complex environmental factors on the GNSS-based train positioning. The simulation results emphasize that signal interferences (i.e., interfered by AM, FM, and pulse signals) have a significant impact on the GNSS-based positioning accuracy, with FM interference exerting the most pronounced effects, particularly in the vertical direction. Environmental factors like terrain obstructions and tunnel shielding further degrade signal visibility and increase po- sitioning errors. The model also identifies critical challenges during signal reacquisition, particularly in tunnel transitions, which exacerbate error growth. While the proposed approach effectively demonstrates the impact of various signal interferences and environmental conditions on the per- formance of the GNSS-based train positioning, future research could explore the integration of additional signal interference mitigation techniques to improve the accuracy and stability of the GNSS-based positioning for railway applications. Principle of length-based train integrity monitoring based on GNSS and trackmap. Due to the existence of positioning error, especially the uncertainty of EOT positioning resulted by the constrained satellite visibility, the uncertainty of the derived train length has to be concerned [12]. As shown in Fig. 6, an uncertainty buffer has to be added to the estimated HOT and EOT position to form an enhanced “envelope” for the train integrity determination purpose. A conservative evaluation of the un- certainty ranges is necessary due to the safety assurance target, and thus several factors related to the positioning performance have to be investigated, including the measurement noise, state estimation error and the potential fault(s) and failure(s). With this consideration, the derived train length would be larger than truth, and thus the threshold for train integrity identification has to be effectively determined to avoid false alarm to the abnormal train integrity events. The uncertainties in HOT and EOT positioning results lead to the es- timation deviation of the train length. When the quality of GNSS posi- tioning degrades with the change of the observation condition, the de- viation of train length estimation would be large and thus the failure of accurate train integrity monitoring may occur. Therefore, the consistency evaluation of the HOT/EOT relative speed can be involved to consolidate the integrity status identification through multiple information resources. Considering the errors in position and speed measurements of the both ends, it would be necessary to constrain the errors through the filtering operation. It can be found that there are mainly two specific trends to the monitoring variables, including relative static and relative acceleration according to the HOT-EOT-based train length and relative speed estimation. Therefore, a sliding window strategy can be involved for mean filtering, and then the filtered data in the window can be fitted by LS-based linear fitting. According to the fitting results, the qualita- tive trend of the data in the sliding window can be recognized to ana- lyze the changing status of the HOT/EOT relative speed and the esti- mation deviation of the train length. 4.2. content.title: Study on key technologies of GNSS-based train state perception for train-centric railway signaling
content.abstract_or_snippet: . Safe train envelope estimation For GNSS-based train positioning and train integrity monitoring, the uncertainty in state estimation has to be concerned to ensure the safety and efficiency of train operation by utilizing the integrity monitoring result. Under the moving block train control scheme, where the occu- pancy of the track segments is determined by the practical length of the train, it is necessary to add a reasonable margin to the raw HOT and EOT location to form a safe occupancy range [13]. The safe train position has to cover the most unfavorable conditions. With this consideration, the margin should be sufficiently large to realize a high possibility to protect the unsatisfied situations. However, an excessive margin will lead to unreasonable identification of track occupancy, which may greatly af- fect the operational efficiency. To realize the expected balance level between the safety and efficiency characteristics, a constrained safe margin has to be adopted on the basis of safety and rationality, so as to reduce the train tracing interval and improve the system efficiency. The essence of the safe train envelope is to realize the safety as- surance of train control by describing the reliability of train positioning. The safe train envelope extends the positioning output based on the evaluation of the uncertainty of positioning errors, and provides a safe overlay to the most possible position derived by the HOT/EOT positioning unit. The safe margin consists of the HPL by integrity monitoring result and the confidential interval of the positioning error. The HPL, which indicates the radius of a circle in the horizontal plane with its center being at the true position to describe the region assured to contain the indicated horizontal position, is usually regarded as the safety boundary of positioning result by GNSS. However, the existence of the integrity risk may lead to an unbounded position error that exceeds the HPL. Without the high-level referencing system during the dynamic operation, it is difficult to accurately evaluate the relia- bility of the safety boundary by HPL. In order to guarantee the cred- ibility of the safe margin for envelope evaluation, it is necessary to estimate the possible range of the positioning error by a specific error confidence interval strategy. Considering the track-constrained along- track operation of railway trains, the error confidence interval indicates a one-dimensional along-track interval that can be represented by the uncertainties in state prediction, measurement update and the trackmap-based location determination, which means (4) and where confidence interval at instant k, result, geographic information from the trackmap database. represent the lower and upper limits of the indicates the user measurement represents the prior denotes the prediction result, and

The main contributions of this paper are as follows. 1) A novel CPN-based modeling framework for GNSS-based train po- sitioning systems is developed. 2) Dynamic influences of signal interferences (i.e., by AM signals, FM signals, pulse signals) and environmental obstructions (i.e., by mountains and tunnels) are incorporated into the model. 3) The performance of the GNSS-based train positioning system is quantitatively evaluated under various scenarios with the CPN model of the system. 2. Related works Over the past decades, GNSS-based systems have been extensively ap- plied to train positioning due to their global coverage and high accuracy under favorable conditions [1,7]. Despite these advantages, challenges such as signal interferences and terrain-induced occlusions continue to pose questions on high positioning accuracy and stability. Numerous studies have explored techniques to mitigate the impacts of signal interferences on GNSS- based positioning accuracy [8–12]. For example, Ref. [9] investigates the impact of continuous wave interference on GNSS receiver tracking perfor- mance, examining the extent of its influence by varying data bit durations or integration times. To address interference-related challenges, an inter- ference energy detection method and a comprehensive orientation tech- nology tailored for GNSS are introduced in Ref. [11]. Besides, a number of researches have examined the impact of environmental factors along railway lines on GNSS signal integrity. For instance, Ref. [13] explores methods of scenario-based weighted measurement noise calculation and fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua- tion in autonomous train positioning systems. Some studies have achieved high positioning accuracy in partially obstructed or challenging environ- ments by fusing multi-sensor data [6,

content.abstract_or_snippet: 1, with the increase of train-to- train communication distance, the quality of the wireless channel at the receiver continuously decreases. Taking the wireless quality of the receiver as -2 dB in reference [17], the coverage range of the onboard antenna transmission power is about 700 m. 123 130 Fig. 1 The coverage of train-to- train communication Urban Rail Transit (2021) 7(2):128–138 2.2 Train Tracking Interval In the rail transit transportation system, with the develop- ment of precise positioning technology and mobile com- munication technology for trains, moving block control has gradually replaced ﬁxed block control and quasi-movable block control. The target braking point of tracking train is set as the rear of the front vehicle to dynamically control the train operation. Based on the train tracking distance in the absolute distance braking mode, this section regards the speed of the front train as 0, that is, the tail-place of the front train as the end point of mobile authorization, and demonstrates the feasibility of train-to-train communica- tion between adjacent trains. The train operation scene in the absolute distance braking mode is shown in Fig. 2. Lb is the service braking distance corresponding to the current running speed of the tracking train; Ls is the safety protection distance between the tracking train and the train ahead; Lmin is the minimum tracking distance of two trains in this mode. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 116–124 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article , Wenzheng Qiaoa, Baigen Caia,b,c, Jian Wanga,b,c, Jiang Liua,b,c, Yuchen Zhaoa, Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways Debiao Lua,b,c,⁎ Chunjie Qiaod, Dirk Spiegele a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c Beijing Engineering Center for EMC and GNSS Technologies, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China d Hunan Matrix Electronic Technology Co., Ltd., Changsha 410205, China e DB InfraGO AG, Berlin 10557, Germany ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS testing Railway train localization 3D environment modelling Digital track map Zero on-site testing The integration of Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) technology into railway train control systems is a crucial step toward achieving the vision of a digital railway. Traditional train control systems undergo extensive in-house tests and prolonged field tests for certification and approval before operational deployment, leading to high costs, delays, and operational disruptions. This paper introduces a GNSS-based train control localization framework which eliminates the need for on-site testing by leveraging train movement dynamics and 3D en- vironment modeling to create a zero on-site testing platform. The proposed framework simulates train move- ment and the surrounding 3D environment using collected railway line location data and environmental attri- butes to generate realistic multipath signals and obscuration effects. This approach enables comprehensive laboratory-based case studies for train localization, reducing the huge amount test of needed for physical field trials. The framework is established in house, using the data collected at the Test Base of China Academy of Railway Sciences (Circular Railway). Results from the open area and cutting environment tests demonstrate high localization accuracy repeatability within the simulated environment, validating the feasibility and effectiveness of zero on-site testing for GNSS-based train control systems. This research highlights the potential of GNSS simulation platforms in enhancing cost efficiency, operational safety, and accuracy for future digital railways.

High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 Fig. 8. The positioning errors and the velocity errors for the normal tracking solution and the INS-aided solution in scenario II. 5. Conclusion In this paper, a GNSS jamming protection solution is presented based on the INS-aided deep integration solution. The solution enables a self-contained PVT reference that is immune to RF signals. INS in- formation is sent to the receiver’s baseband, realizing the assistance to the receiver at the signal processing level. The measured velocity in- formation from INS is mapped to the LOS between the satellite and the vehicle to obtain the Doppler of relative motion. The Doppler is fed back to the NCO in the tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The the proposed solution demonstrates significant evaluation of improvements in positioning and velocity, enabling resilient train po- sitioning for securing next generation GNSS-based train control sys- tems. Future work will focus on refining mitigation techniques with extended studies for multi-constellation scenarios (e.g., BeiDou and Galileo), and exploring field tests in real lines to ensure the resilience of GNSS-based train localization systems in railway applications. Through the integration of static trackmap information, sensor data fusion for BDS/GPS re- ceivers, INS sensors and the odometer measurements were real- timely achieved with a high availability level under the complex and time-changing operation environment. Both the static and dynamic test cases were involved in the field demonstration. Table 6 sum- marizes the accuracy results of train positioning under different system modes. The results from field demonstration illustrate that involvement of the presented key technologies, including the safe platform and multi- source sensor fusion mechanism, effectively guarantees the perfor- mance of GNSS-based autonomous train positioning and state percep- tion even under complex and time-varying operational conditions. The utilization of the differential GNSS technology and the integrity mon- itoring logic makes it possible of achieving the high accuracy and ro- bustness against the possible difficult or even critical conditions. The field test results demonstrate the feasibility and the advantages of utilizing BDS in realizing a novel autonomous train control system with a train-centric design, which is of great significance in the future high- speed and plateau railway applications with the enhanced environ- mental flexibility and whole life cycle cost efficiency. 7. Conclusion The application of GNSS technology is one of the representative characteristics of intelligent railway systems in the future. However, there are specific requirements to the GNSS-based train positioning and state perception in the railway signaling applications due to the critical re- quirement to the safety. Thus, the utilization of GNSS has to be enhanced by the specific technical solutions. The sensor data fusion technique is of great significance to enhance the service availability under degraded or even a critical GNSS observing condition. To ensure the compatibility to the existing system specifications, the VB technology enables the in- tegration of the GNSS capabilities into the train control system and meanwhile the train integrity monitoring logic allows the opportunity to realize moving block-based train operation control through the dynamic identification of the track occupancy and safe envelope of the train. By investigating the relationship between the GNSS performance system and the RAMS specification, the performance evaluation of the GNSS-based train positioning can be effectively realized. It can be found that the in- volvement of these key technologies consolidates the autonomy and cap- abilities of the train-borne system, which releases the requirement to the track-side facilities like track circuit and Balises. Introduction Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) have seen vast applications in the transportation domain. The integration of Global Positioning System (GPS) and Beidou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) in railway is efficient for modernizing the railway network, GNSS is increasingly becoming a fundamental pillar not only for non-safety applications, but also for im- proved maintenance, automated train operation, and safety- related appli- cations, including the Next Generation of Train Control (NGTC) and re- novation of the train on-board equipment, all these will increase the railway system capacity, efficiency and safety of all railways [1]. To apply GNSS in railway train operation, the GNSS performance testing and verification is the essential procedure for the system to be adopted and further listed as mandatory in the system design and development. The certification of the GNSS-based train localization systems is also necessary to provide concrete validation or testing application services to carry out simulation tests with evaluation of the platform and environmental condi- tion simulations [2]. Beidou has a set of technical and regulatory standards, including the BDS Performance Standard, BDS Signal Interface Control Document (ICD), and technical requirements for user equipment. The BDS Signal In Space (SIS) and service performance characteristics include ac- curacy, availability, compatibility, and interoperability [3]. GPS has also published standard positioning service performance standards to guide the user for civil uses worldwide, the performance metrics are GPS SIS accuracy, continuity, and availability [4]. So both BDS and GPS are focusing on ac- curacy as the foundation for the GNSS performance [5]. The performance needs to be assessed and as the performances are all credited to the equipment, the equipment itself needs to be certified. The certification procedure, it is governed by various international and regional standards, such as the accuracy standards by BDS and GPS, the test and simulation standard procedures, the simulation must meet possible (as sensible to the GNSS) to make the test results plausible. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex- tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems. Early in the 1990s, the United States introduces the concept of the Positive Train control System (PTS) [1], which relies on Global Posi- tioning System (GPS), accelerometers, and fiber-optic gyroscopes for the train positioning function. By December 2020, the United States achieved 100 % implementation of Positive Train Control (PTC) across all required railroads, marking a historic milestone in rail safety and interoperability [2]. CLUG 2.0 project [3] launched in 2023 in Europe aims to enhance the European Rail Traffic Management System (ERTMS) by integrating an on-board GNSS+EGNOS-based multi-sensor fusion architecture. In China, the China State Railway Group Co., Ltd. introduced a new train control system, the Chinese Train Control System-New (CTCS-N) [4], which proposed a “train-borne centric” train operation control method. This system incorporates on-board GNSS positioning, speed sensors, and electronic railway maps to achieve train localization. GNSS has been acknowledged as a pivotal enabler in im- proving the cost efficiency and automation level of the train control system. In complex railway operation scenarios, such as urban canyons, tunnels, and stations with cover, the limited GNSS signal visibility, and weak observation geometry would greatly decrease the positioning performance. Thus, the integration solutions are typically proposed for the train safety positioning. A typical integration solution for GNSS- based train positioning is based on the data fusion with both GNSS and Inertial Navigation System (INS) sensors. The GNSS/INS integrated solution is capable of providing full navigation solutions of position, velocity, and attitude in high positioning accuracy, high updating rate even under the GNSS limited scenario when the visible satellites are less than four. Additionally, there is an increasing concern that GNSS is also vulnerable to radio frequency jamming, due to the weak signal power of the GNSS signal. More and more GNSS disturbance events are reported because of jamming, in worldwide, including maritime applications [5,6], aviation applications [7], and also railway applications [8,9]. GNSS in-band interferences, for example, intentional jamming shows a source of potential threats to the safe, secure, and resilient operation of a dense railway system utilizing GNSS as the main positioning method

content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning Zhuojian Caoa, Jiang Liua,b,c,⁎ a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b State Key Laboratory of Advanced Rail Autonomous Operation, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China , Wei Jianga,b,c, Baigen Caia,b,c ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS jamming Deeply-coupled integration GNSS/INS Railway safety and efficiency increasingly rely on precise train positioning. The integration of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) into railway control systems aims to reduce dependence on track-side in- frastructure. While GNSS has significantly improved train localization, challenges such as the susceptibility to jamming remain. To address this, this paper introduces an Inertial Navigation System (INS)-aided train posi- tioning system based on deep integration, exploring its performance through semi-physical experiments and simulations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed solution is able to reduce the positioning error by 63.47 %, and the velocity error by 58.47 % under jamming conditions. The study highlights the potential of deep integration for improving the resilience of GNSS-based train control systems, especially in the face of Radio Frequency (RF) jamming threats. 1. Introduction Railway safety and efficiency depend increasingly on knowing every train’s position. Traditional train positioning technologies usually in- volve track circuit positioning, axle counter positioning, and intersec- tion inductive loop positioning. However, these methods are often limited by insufficient positioning accuracy and high maintenance costs. The application of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) in railway train control systems aims to reduce as much as possible the use of track-side infrastructures for train location awareness, including track circuits and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex- tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals, which makes it possible to benefit from the mitigation of GNSS jamming signals. In the railway domain, an extensive analysis of interference events based on a one-year dataset collected across the Swiss railway network was con- ducted in Ref. [8], which revealed widespread occurrences of GNSS interference, particularly clustered around larger cities and densely populated residential areas. A test and evaluation of GNSS-based railway positioning under various jamming conditions was presented in Ref. [17], demonstrating the susceptibility of train localization perfor- mance to interference. A resilient localization framework operating at the measurement level, incorporating jamming discrimination and multi-sensor fusion, was proposed in Ref. [18]. Nevertheless, research on GNSS anti-jamming solutions for railway environments remains re- latively limited, which motivates the focus of the present work. GNSS/INS integration can be categorized into three primary archi- tectures: loosely coupled, tightly coupled, and deeply coupled sys- tems [19,20]. In a loosely coupled integration, the GNSS receiver and INS operate independently, with integration occurring at the navigation solution level by combining the position and velocity outputs from both systems. This approach requires a minimum of four visible satellites for a positioning solution, which can be a limitation in environments with jamming signals. In contrast, tightly-coupled integration integrates raw GNSS measurements, such as pseudoranges and Doppler shifts, directly with INS solution within a filter. This method allows the system to utilize all available satellite signals, even when fewer than four sa- tellites are visible, enhancing performance in challenging environ- ments. Deeply coupled, or ultra-tight, integration goes a step further by incorporating inertial measurements directly into the GNSS signal tracking loops. This integration aids in maintaining signal lock under high dynamics and interference conditions, as the INS can provide predictive information to the GNSS receiver’s tracking algorithms. The advantages of deep integration are particularly evident in environments with significant interference or high dynamics. In Ref. [21], the anti- jamming performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-j

content.abstract_or_snippet: will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. GNSS/INS/Odometer sensor data fusion The ODO (Odometer) is the most commonly used train positioning method in existing train control systems. Wheel slide and slip during the train operation, as well as wheel diameter wear, may result in errors in the train speed measurement, and lead to error accumulation in the along-track location calculation results. Such errors will be brought into the INS/ODO integrated positioning system. The train positioning error by INS/ODO drifts over time until the position calibration results are available. Different from the Balises, results of GNSS positioning can provide the correction without relying on the track-side. Performance of a GNSS/INS/ODO integrated positioning system will be determined by the reliability and accuracy of GNSS. When the GNSS performance decreases or the GNSS signal is blocked for a long time, the result of INS will not be guaranteed effectively [6]. Therefore, the availability and observation quality of GNSS greatly affect the precision and effective- ness of train positioning using INS and ODO. The key issue in realizing the multi-sensor integrated train positioning is the sensor fusion logic. It is in charge of combining the measurements from multiple sensors, improving the reliability and fault tolerance of the integrated system, providing suitable auxiliary information for the ex- isting location determination system, and simplifying the system com- plexity over those track-side-relied solutions. Among different sensor fu- sion algorithms, Federated Kalman Filter (FKF) is a distributed cascaded integrated sensor fusion method and widely considered in applications because of its flexibility, low computational effort, and excellent fault tolerance capability. FKF consists of several local filters and a main filter, in which the system information is allocated in the local filters and then fused in the main filter. For the GNSS/INS/ODO integrated train posi- tioning system based on the FKF, INS is integrated due to its advantages in high output rate, autonomy and reliability. Considering the INS-related local filtering structures, two local filters for GNSS/INS and INS/ODO can 48 Fig. 1. Architecture of GNSS/INS/ODO sensor data fusion for integrated train positioning. be established, as shown in Fig. 1. Based on this traditional tightly-coupled GNSS/INS integra- tion, the estimated navigation states are used to estimate the Doppler shift information, which is fed back to the GNSS tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The system operates in a closed-loop way where the integrated filter continuously updates the system’s state estimation by combining data from both the GNSS and INS, and the feedback of the estimated states helps improve the accuracy of both the INS and GNSS calculations. The INS-aided deep integration system leverages the in- ertial data, effectively narrowing the bandwidth of the tracking loop, which helps reduce the noise within the loop and suppress external jamming signals. The system’s capability to maintain accurate tracking and position estimation in jamming environments is significantly strengthened, leading to a more resilient navigation solution. 3. Jamming analysis and protection 3.1. Jamming analysis The received signal strength of the GNSS receiver is extremely low and falls well below the thermal noise floor. Typically, the received GPS signal levels fall within the range of −127 dBm [22]. The weak char- acteristics of GNSS signals will lead to an increasing risk of performance degradation under jamming conditions. Jamming signals may emanate from telecommunication and electronic systems operating in adjacent bands or in bands relatively far from the GNSS bands, i.e., Fre- quency Modulation (FM)/TV transmitter harmonics, Amplitude Mod- ulation (AM) transmitters, and mobile phone networks. The digital re- presentation of the received signal in the presence of jamming at the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) output is (1)

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-jamming methods remains constrained by simple jamming models such as con- tinuous wave jamming. The mechanisms under different jamming modulation schemes remain underexplored. This study expands the exploration of jamming types by focusing on sweep-frequency inter- ference. Considering the compatibility of the GNSS/INS deeply-coupled integration with the current GNSS-based train positioning function in the control system conception, this paper proposes an INS-aided train determination system (LDS) based on the deep integration. The system profile and the GNSS jamming signals are briefly introduced firstly. Then, we give a detailed introduction of deeply-coupled integration design. Finally, a hardware simulator is used and semi-physical ex- periments are conducted to assess the proposed system’s performance with a sweep frequency jamming scenario. 2. System design From Fig. 1, it can be found that multiple information resources are involved in the train positioning system, where the inertial data from the accelerometers and gyroscopes is taken into the Position, Velocity, and Timing (PVT) calculation to enhance the resilience to abnormal jamming attacks. By integrating an INS directly into the train onboard subsystem, it becomes unnecessary to install or modify the track-side subsystem, thereby simplifying system design and maintenance, thereby providing a flexible architecture for modern rail train control systems. From a signal processing perspective, jamming signals are collected and analyzed together with the authentic GNSS signals by the antenna and the subsequent front-end stage in the GNSS receiver simulta- neously. The signal contaminated by jamming signals would directly affect the subsequent stages of the receiver’s processing chain, in- cluding acquisition and tracking stages. In the tracking stage, jamming prevents the code and carrier loops from maintaining stable signal tracking, ultimately resulting in pronounced errors or even failure in the PVT solution. In the proposed system, the Doppler shift information derived from INS is fed back to aid the GNSS tracking loop, improving the overall resilience of the navigation solution. Inertial mechanization is used to infer Inertial Solution at discrete epochs, while the GNSS tracking loop operates simultaneously until they meet the integration epoch. updated_hypotheses.statement: Im Bahnkontext ist GNSS allein in komplexen Umgebungen und bei Funkstörungen nicht hinreichend robust; insbesondere Tunnel, Einschnitte/Half-sky-Szenarien, Terrainabschattung und AM/FM/Puls-Interferenzen verschlechtern Sichtbarkeit, Signalverfolgung und Positionsgenauigkeit bis hin zum vollständigen Ausfall im Tunnel.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Hypothese passt sehr gut zur Evidenz: Mehrere bahnspezifische Dokumente beschreiben konsistent reduzierte Sichtbarkeit in Half-sky/Cutting/Tunnel-Szenarien, vollständige Blockade im Tunnel sowie deutliche Genauigkeitsverluste und Reakquisitionsfehler nach Tunnelausfahrt. Zusätzlich werden AM/FM/Puls-Interferenzen als signifikante Ursache für Tracking- und Genauigkeitsdegradation genannt. Ein belastbarer Widerspruch ist in den bereitgestellten Dokumenten nicht erkennbar.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Die Integration von INS erhöht die Resilienz der Zugortung, weil inertiale Messungen GNSS-Ausfälle, reduzierte Satellitensichtbarkeit und Jamming auf System- bzw. Tracking-Ebene überbrücken; besonders tief gekoppelte GNSS/INS-Architekturen stabilisieren die Signalverfolgung und senken Fehler unter Störbedingungen deutlich.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Evidenz stützt die Hypothese substanziell: INS ist RF-unabhängig und wird in tief gekoppelten Architekturen direkt zur Doppler-/Tracking-Unterstützung genutzt, was unter Jamming die Resilienz erhöht. Semi-physische Experimente berichten deutliche Fehlerreduktionen; Feldtests mit GNSS/INS/ODO-Fusion zeigen verbesserte dynamische Positionierung. Der Falsifikationsversuch ergibt nur eine Randbedingung: Bei langen GNSS-Ausfällen driftet INS/ODO, daher ersetzt INS GNSS nicht unbegrenzt, was aber der Hypothese nicht widerspricht.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Für ausfallsicheren digitalen Bahnbetrieb genügt GNSS/INS-Genauigkeit allein nicht; erforderlich sind eisenbahnspezifische Integritätsüberwachung, sichere HOT/EOT-Zughüllen mit HPL/Konfidenzintervallen sowie RAMS-orientierte Verfügbarkeits- und Sicherheitsnachweise.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Hypothese ist gut gestützt: Die Dokumente zeigen, dass Positionsunsicherheit sicherheitsrelevant in HOT/EOT-Zughüllen eingeht und explizit HPL sowie Konfidenzintervalle für sichere Belegungsbestimmung verwendet werden. Zudem wird die Bewertung von GNSS-basierter Zugzustandserfassung an die RAMS-Architektur angebunden. Feldnachweise betonen Integritätslogik und Verfügbarkeit als Voraussetzung für robuste Anwendungen. Keine bereitgestellten Dokumente legen nahe, dass reine Genauigkeit ohne Integrität/RAMS-Nachweis ausreichen würde.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Die praktische Einführung von GNSS/INS in der digitalen Schiene wird wesentlich durch Validierungs-, Test- und Zertifizierungsanforderungen begrenzt; daher gewinnen standardisierte Labor- und Zero-on-site-Testplattformen mit 3D-Umgebungsmodellierung und Digital Track Maps an Bedeutung, um Feldtests kostengünstiger und risikoärmer zu ersetzen bzw. vorzubereiten.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Evidenz passt gut: Test- und Zertifizierungsaufwand wird als essenziell und kosten-/risikointensiv beschrieben; gleichzeitig werden standardisierte Labor- bzw. Zero-on-site-Ansätze mit 3D-Umgebungsmodellierung und Digital Track Maps als Mittel genannt, um Feldtests vorzubereiten oder teilweise zu ersetzen. Ein klarer Gegenbeleg gegen die Bedeutung dieser Plattformen liegt nicht vor. Die Aussage zur praktischen Einführung ist plausibel und dokumentennah, auch wenn der langfristige Zertifizierungsumfang über die bereitgestellten Auszüge hinaus weiter untersucht werden könnte.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

challenge_findings.description: Mehrere Dokumente bestätigen, dass Tunnel, Half-sky/Cutting-Szenarien, Terrainabschattung sowie AM/FM/Puls-Interferenzen die GNSS-Sichtbarkeit, Signalverfolgung und Positionsgenauigkeit im Bahnkontext deutlich verschlechtern; im Tunnel kann die Positionierung vollständig ausfallen.

INS-gestützte, insbesondere tief gekoppelte GNSS/INS-Architekturen stabilisieren Tracking-Loops durch Doppler-Aiding und verbessern unter Jamming die Positions- und Geschwindigkeitsbestimmung deutlich.
Die Evidenz zeigt eine wichtige Randbedingung: INS/ODO-Lösungen driften bei längeren GNSS-Ausfällen, sodass INS die GNSS-Funktion nicht beliebig lange autonom ersetzen kann.

Für sichere Zugortung reichen Rohpositionswerte nicht aus; HOT/EOT-Zughüllen, HPL, Konfidenzintervalle und RAMS-bezogene Integritäts- und Verfügbarkeitsmetriken werden explizit als sicherheitsrelevant beschrieben.

Die Dokumente beschreiben, dass Validierung, Verifikation und Zertifizierung von GNSS-basierter Zuglokalisierung aufwendig sind und dass Zero-on-site-Testplattformen mit 3D-Modellierung und Digital Track Maps die Prüfung reproduzierbarer, kostengünstiger und risikoärmer machen sollen.
